summary: The Seattle Sounders instituted a "vote of confidence" system in which season ticket holders vote every four years on whether the general manager keeps his job, modeled on European club membership structures like Barcelona and Real Madrid. Hanauer, who was general manager at the time, went through the process himself after the 2012 season and won reappointment because the team had had a good year. The mechanism is not symbolic — a losing vote would actually cost the GM the job — which is unusual for North American pro sports ownership structures that are typically closed and unaccountable to fans.
quote: every four years our fans our season ticket holders get to vote on whether the general manager retains uh his or her job or loses it it's a vote of confidence or no confidence we've been through the process once i believe it was after the 2012 season um i was the general manager at the time thankfully we had a good season in 2012 and uh got a vote of of confidence

summary: Despite chairing the process himself and having engineered the fan-vote system, Hanauer admits the vote of confidence was genuinely nerve-wracking for him personally — to the point that his own brother told him he didn't think he'd get re-elected. This shows the accountability mechanism he built isn't just PR theater; it created real personal stakes and uncertainty even for the insider running the club, undercutting the assumption that owner-designed "fan democracy" initiatives are safely engineered to never threaten the people who create them.
quote: it was a little nerve-wracking especially when my brother told me didn't think i'd get re-elected

summary: The Sounders' "democracy in sports" fan-ownership model — built around the GM confidence vote and quarterly fan advisory councils — wasn't derived from internal strategy discussions but was imported wholesale by co-owner Drew Carey after he personally experienced membership-club culture at Barcelona and Real Madrid and loved it. The origin is idiosyncratic (one owner's personal travel experience) rather than a calculated business decision, which explains why the model reads more like a values statement ("we wanted fans to feel like stakeholders, not ATMs") than a data-driven customer-engagement strategy.
quote: it was really driven if i'm honest by uh drew carey who's one of one of my partners uh who had been to uh to barcelona and rail madrid and and love that uh love that system uh and so we created you know our mantra i guess is democracy in sports

summary: Hanauer contrasts running the Sounders with running a "normal" business where monthly or quarterly financials give a steady read on whether things are going well. In pro sports, he says the real P&L is the result on Saturday — a team can do everything right for weeks in a row and still have the outcome hinge on a ball deflecting a millimeter one way (a goal) or a meter the other way (a save off the post), with fans and press judging the whole operation off that single random bounce. This frames performance measurement in sports as inherently noisy and disconnected from process quality, unlike most businesses where effort and outcomes correlate more directly.
quote: for us our p l really is the results on saturday and you can do everything right for week after week after week and the ball you know one millimeter to the right and it's a goal one meter to the left it bounces out off the post and everyone thinks you're an idiot

summary: Managing celebrity co-owners Joe Roth (a Hollywood producer) and Drew Carey works in practice through very different, specific rhythms rather than one generic "absentee owner" relationship: Roth, based in LA, talks to Hanauer daily or a couple of times a day and attends roughly half the team's games despite the distance, while Carey is lower-key and less present at games as his LA work has increased. The specificity of daily/twice-daily contact and roughly half-of-games attendance shows how deeply involved these "celebrity" owners actually stay day to day, contrary to an assumption that famous minority owners are largely disengaged figureheads.
quote: we talk if not i mean every day uh if not a couple times a day um always wants to know what's going on with the team ... he comes to probably half the games maybe more

summary: On the sports-science side, every Sounders player wears a heart-rate monitor and a GPS unit during every training session, letting staff correlate acceleration, deceleration, distance covered, and speed with heart-rate data tied to specific drills. As a concrete example, Hanauer cites a 5v5 small-sided training game in which Clint Dempsey's heart rate hit 97% of his max early in the session because he hadn't been running at that speed for an extended period — a level of granular, player-specific physiological data that goes well beyond a generic "we use sports science" claim.
quote: we know you know during a 5v5 uh small sided game that you know dempsey's heart rate got to 97 percent of his max heart rate um in the early stage of this this you know of this exercise where he hadn't run it at speed for very like

summary: Hanauer frames the club's approach to VR investment as a specific cost tradeoff rather than a vague "we're excited about VR" position: he expects adoption to start on the team/coaching side first because the club is willing to spend around half a million dollars on a system that helps performance analysis, versus needing tens of thousands of individual fans (he estimates 60,000) to each buy a Hololens-type headset before fan-facing VR becomes viable. This concrete cost comparison — one large institutional purchase versus tens of thousands of small consumer purchases — is his actual reasoning for why team-side VR will arrive before fan-side VR, not just a generic prediction.
quote: i think it'll probably start um uh more on the uh coach on the on the team side people who you know where we are willing to invest half a million dollars in a system that that will help us as opposed to 60 000 uh fans at home with hololens sets watching

summary: On broadcast strategy, the Sounders have deliberately turned down higher-paying rights deals in favor of staying on wide, free, over-the-air distribution (naming their partner as Q13/Joe TV) because it maximizes fan access and audience-building rather than short-term rights revenue — while acknowledging there's a dollar figure large enough that would change that calculus. This is a specific, named tradeoff decision (distribution over money) rather than a generic statement about broadcast strategy.
quote: our our broadcasts are on uh q13 joe tv um over the air wide distribution we've had some choices along the way to maybe get paid a little bit more money um but less uh less access and distribution for our fans uh and we've chosen the path of more distribution but there is some amount of money that would convince us to change change course
